The tragedy of this chapter is not that God withheld mercy, but that Israel received it and refused to change. King Jehoahaz, crushed under Aramean oppression, cried out to the LORD. The weight of suffering drove him to seek God's favor, and the LORD heard him—not because Jehoahaz deserved it, but because He saw the affliction of His people. A deliverer came. Israel escaped. They lived in their own homes again. But verses 6 tells us what happened next: they did not turn away from their sins. The Asherah pole remained standing. They had been saved from destruction without being saved from their idolatry. This is the pattern that haunts the northern kingdom—deliverance without transformation, divine mercy met with human obstinacy. God gave them what they needed to survive, but they would not give Him what He deserved: their obedience and worship.

Yet God did not abandon them. When Jehoahaz's son Jehoash reigned, he too walked in the sins of Jeroboam. He too did evil. But God sent the prophet Elisha to him in his final days, and in that deathbed encounter, something remarkable happened. Elisha instructed the king to shoot an arrow toward Aram—a symbolic act declaring that the LORD's victory belonged to Israel. The king shot one arrow. Then Elisha told him to strike the ground with the remaining arrows. Here was a test of faith disguised as obedience. The king struck three times and stopped. Elisha was angry. "You should have struck five or six times," he said. "Then you would have struck down Aram until you had put an end to it. But now you will strike down Aram only three times." The king's hesitation, his half-hearted response, limited what God would do through him. He had the power to act but lacked the conviction to act fully. His weakness became the measure of his victory.

After Elisha died, something extraordinary occurred. When someone's body touched Elisha's bones, the man was revived and stood up. The prophet's power to restore life did not end with his death—a sign that pointed beyond himself to the resurrection authority that belongs to God alone. Yet even this miracle, even the ongoing power of God manifest through His servant, could not compel Israel to turn. The text moves forward relentlessly: Hazael oppressed Israel, then died. His son Ben-hadad took his place. Jehoash defeated Ben-hadad three times and recovered the cities his father had lost. Three times—exactly as many as he had struck the ground. His limited faith produced limited victory. Yet in all of this, verse 23 reveals what sustains everything: "The LORD was gracious to Israel and had compassion on them, and He turned toward them because of His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob." God's faithfulness to His ancient promises kept Israel from utter destruction, not their merit or their repentance, but His covenant love alone. Israel lived not because they deserved to live, but because God's Word, sworn centuries before, would not fail.
